original_hypothesis.title: Broken onlyOwner/onlyManager Modifiers Enable Complete Protocol Takeover
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: Security.sol modifiers onlyOwner and onlyManager evaluate boolean expressions as no-op statements (`msg.sender == owner;` and `managers[msg.sender] == true;`) without require/revert. Every function guarded by these modifiers is effectively publicly callable by any address. An attacker can call transferOwnership to become owner, setManager to become manager, setProtocolFeePercent to redirect all protocol fees, setFeeRedistributor to a malicious contract, and setERC20Factory to deploy backdoored tokens.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: This is a fundamental access control failure affecting all privileged operations across Curves.sol and FeeSplitter.sol. The modifiers compile without error but provide zero protection.

proof_of_signal.pattern: modifier onlyOwner() { msg.sender == owner; _; } — boolean expression as statement with no require/if-revert, compiles as no-op

preconditions: Any external address can call these functions
No additional guards exist in the modifier bodies

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Boolean expression as statement in modifier body
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: msg.sender == owner evaluates to true/false but result is discarded; execution continues to _;

analysis_questions: Can Solidity 0.8.7 compiler warn about unused expression results in modifiers?
Are there any other guards in the call chain that might compensate?
What is the complete list of exploitable functions?
validation_requirements: Call transferOwnership from non-owner address and verify success
Call setProtocolFeePercent from non-owner and verify state change

original_hypothesis.preliminary_attack_scenario: Attacker calls transferOwnership(attacker), then setProtocolFeePercent(1 ether, attacker) to set 100% protocol fee to their address. All subsequent trades route ETH to attacker.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Complete protocol fund drainage — all future trade fees, ability to deploy malicious factory/redistributor

original_hypothesis.test_coverage_gap: No test verifying onlyOwner/onlyManager actually revert for unauthorized callers

validation_result.impact_summary: All onlyOwner and onlyManager protected functions are publicly callable by any address, enabling complete protocol takeover including ownership transfer, fee redirection, and factory replacement.
validation_result.detailed_analysis: Security.sol defines two modifiers, onlyOwner (lines 8-11) and onlyManager (lines 13-16), that evaluate boolean comparison expressions as standalone statements. In Solidity, `msg.sender == owner;` is a valid statement that evaluates to true or false, but the result is discarded — it is not wrapped in require() or used in an if-revert pattern. The modifier body then proceeds to `_;`, executing the function body unconditionally regardless of the caller's identity. This is a fundamental access control failure affecting every function in Curves.sol and FeeSplitter.sol that relies on these modifiers.
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: Direct examination of Security.sol lines 8-16 confirms the defect. The modifier bodies contain bare boolean expressions (`msg.sender == owner;` and `managers[msg.sender] == true;`) with no require, assert, or if-revert guard. The Solidity 0.8.7 compiler compiles these as no-ops — the comparison is evaluated and immediately discarded. The `_;` placeholder then executes the function body unconditionally.

vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: The expression `msg.sender == owner;` evaluates a boolean comparison but discards the result. It should be `require(msg.sender == owner);` to enforce the access control check. Without require, execution always proceeds to `_;`.

The expression `managers[msg.sender] == true;` evaluates a boolean comparison but discards the result. It should be `require(managers[msg.sender] == true);` or `require(managers[msg.sender]);`. Without require, execution always proceeds to `_;`.
This function is intended to be owner-only but the onlyOwner modifier is a no-op. Any address can call this to become the owner.
Any address can call this to grant or revoke manager status for any address.
Any address can call this to redirect all protocol fees to an attacker-controlled address.
Any address can replace the fee redistributor with a malicious contract.
Any address can replace the ERC20 factory with a malicious factory that deploys backdoored tokens.
Any address can call this to reset any user's fee offset, destroying their accumulated unclaimed fees.

calling_sequence: 1. Attacker calls transferOwnership(attackerAddress) on the Curves contract
2. onlyOwner modifier executes: `msg.sender == owner;` evaluates to false (discarded)
3. Modifier proceeds to `_;`, executing the function body
4. `owner = owner_;` sets owner to attacker's address
5. Attacker is now the owner (though ownership check doesn't matter since modifiers are no-ops)
6. Attacker calls setProtocolFeePercent to redirect all protocol fees
7. Attacker calls setFeeRedistributor to set malicious fee splitter
8. Attacker calls setERC20Factory to deploy backdoored tokens
execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: The vulnerability is at the modifier level in Security.sol:8-16. Every function path through onlyOwner or onlyManager is affected. The modifier evaluates `msg.sender == owner` (or `managers[msg.sender] == true`) as a statement expression — the comparison returns a bool but the result is not consumed by require() or used in a conditional. Solidity compiles this to a SLOAD + EQ that pushes a result onto the stack, which is then immediately popped (POP opcode). The `_;` then executes the function body unconditionally. This affects: Security.sol:setManager (line 24), Security.sol:transferOwnership (line 28), Curves.sol:setFeeRedistributor (line 113), Curves.sol:setMaxFeePercent (line 117), Curves.sol:setProtocolFeePercent (line 128), Curves.sol:setExternalFeePercent (line 141), Curves.sol:setERC20Factory (line 155), FeeSplitter.sol:addFees (line 93), FeeSplitter.sol:onBalanceChange (line 99).

config_validation.deployment_evidence: No deployment scripts provided that could add compensating controls. The local-setup.ts imports deployContracts and postDeploy from ../deployment/deploy which is not in scope. However, the vulnerability is in the contract source code itself — no deployment script can fix a broken modifier.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: Attack is trivially executable: (1) Any EOA calls Curves.transferOwnership(attackerAddr) — succeeds because onlyOwner is a no-op. (2) Attacker calls setProtocolFeePercent(maxAllowedFee, attackerAddr) to redirect protocol fees. (3) Attacker calls setFeeRedistributor(maliciousContract) to control fee distribution. (4) Attacker calls setERC20Factory(maliciousFactory) to deploy backdoored ERC20 tokens. (5) Attacker calls FeeSplitter.onBalanceChange(token, victim) to wipe victims' accumulated fees. All these calls succeed in a single transaction each with zero ETH required.
validation_result.impact_assessment: CRITICAL — Complete protocol takeover. All administrative functions are publicly callable. An attacker can: (1) Become owner and manager of all contracts, (2) Redirect all protocol fees to themselves, (3) Replace the fee redistributor to steal holder fees, (4) Replace the ERC20 factory to deploy malicious token contracts, (5) Call onBalanceChange to wipe any user's accumulated fees, (6) Call addFees with arbitrary parameters to manipulate fee distribution. This affects all protocol users and all funds in the system.
recommended_mitigations: Replace `msg.sender == owner;` with `require(msg.sender == owner, "Only owner");` in the onlyOwner modifier
Replace `managers[msg.sender] == true;` with `require(managers[msg.sender] == true, "Only manager");` in the onlyManager modifier
Add onlyOwner modifier to FeeSplitter.setCurves() function
Consider using OpenZeppelin's Ownable and AccessControl contracts instead of custom access control
Add comprehensive access control tests that verify revert on unauthorized calls
validation_result.replication_plan: 1. Deploy Security.sol (or Curves.sol which inherits it) with account A as deployer/owner. 2. From account B (non-owner), call transferOwnership(accountB). 3. Verify owner state variable is now accountB — confirms onlyOwner is bypassed. 4. From account C (non-manager), call setManager(accountC, true). 5. Verify managers[accountC] is true — confirms modifier bypass. 6. From account C, call setProtocolFeePercent with attacker address. 7. Verify feesEconomics.protocolFeeDestination changed — confirms complete takeover.
additional_testing_needed: Verify Solidity 0.8.7 compiler does not emit a warning for unused expression results in modifiers (it likely does not error, only potentially warns)
Test with optimizer enabled (as configured in hardhat.config.ts with runs=1000) to confirm the no-op is not optimized into a revert
